title: Maintaining a compost heap through winter
subtitle: Managing moisture, carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, and insulation to keep microbial activity going below 5 degrees Celsius
excerpt: Microbial activity in a compost heap slows dramatically below 10 degrees Celsius and nearly stops below 5 degrees. A winter heap does not fail; it pauses. The challenge is setting it up so it restarts fast in spring rather than compacting into a wet, anaerobic mass. This guide covers the management steps that maintain partial activity through a UK winter and accelerate spring restart.
sourceNotes: Centre for Alternative Technology guidance on winter composting. RHS advice on winter compost heap management. Composting Association UK member guidance on cold-season operation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of composting material. Browns (high carbon) and greens (high nitrogen) should be balanced at roughly 25:1 to 30:1 for active hot composting.
A managed composting process that maintains temperatures of 55 to 70 degrees Celsius through active turning and balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, decomposing material in 4 to 12 weeks.
Slow, unmanaged composting that proceeds at ambient temperatures, typically taking 12 to 24 months to produce finished compost.

content.text: A
hot compost
heap that was at 55 to 65 degrees Celsius in autumn will cool progressively as air temperature falls in November. Below 10 degrees Celsius the heap is effectively in
cold compost

mode. It decomposes slowly via fungi and cold-tolerant bacteria rather than heat-loving bacteria. The key management goals are to prevent rain saturation, maintain enough carbon to prevent compaction, and insulate the outer layer to retain some warmth.
Preparing the heap before temperatures drop

In October, before the first hard frost, make a final turn of the heap and add a thick layer of browns (cardboard, straw, dry leaves) at least 150 mm deep on top and around the sides. This insulating layer retains heat and prevents rain saturation of the active composting zone. Check the
C:N ratio
: a heap that went into autumn too nitrogen-rich (too many grass clippings, too little cardboard) will go slimy and anaerobic in cold, wet conditions. Add shredded cardboard to correct.
Managing additions through winter
Continue adding kitchen waste throughout winter. Vegetable peelings, fruit, and coffee grounds are all suitable. Each addition adds nitrogen and microbial activity.
Layer every kitchen waste addition with two to three times its volume of cardboard or dry leaves. This maintains the C:N ratio and prevents the surface from becoming wet and compacted.
Do not add large quantities of greens in winter. Grass clippings are not available in winter, but also avoid large volumes of raw cabbage or squash. Both are high in moisture and nitrogen.
Cover the heap with a tarpaulin or a fitted compost bin lid to prevent rain saturation. A waterlogged heap in winter produces acetic acid and hydrogen sulphide rather than compost.
Insulating the heap

A heap insulated on all sides with 150 mm of straw, cardboard, or fleece can maintain an internal temperature 5 to 10 degrees Celsius above ambient. In a UK winter with a mean temperature of 3 to 5 degrees Celsius, the insulated core may hold 8 to 12 degrees: enough for slow fungal activity and some bacterial decomposition. A
compost thermometer

(the tool, not the ratio) inserted into the centre of a 1 m³ heap should read 5 to 15 degrees Celsius in mid-winter in a well-managed heap. Below 5 degrees, the heap is effectively frozen and needs no management until temperatures rise.
Spring restart

When air temperatures consistently exceed 10 degrees Celsius (March to April in most of England), turn the heap completely. Mix the outer insulating layer with the inner composting material. Add fresh greens (early nettles, comfrey, grass as it starts growing) as a nitrogen boost. Within 3 to 7 days of a good turn with added greens, temperatures in a well-structured heap should reach 45 to 55 degrees Celsius. If the heap has been well managed over winter, this restart happens faster than starting from scratch.

attrs.symptom: Heap smells strongly of ammonia or bad eggs in winter

content.text: Anaerobic conditions have developed. The heap has too much nitrogen and too little carbon, or is waterlogged. Add large quantities of cardboard, lift the tarpaulin to aerate, and wait for a dry spell before turning.

Do not turn or add material to a frozen heap. Leave it until the thaw, then assess condition. A heap that freezes completely without insulation is normal in Scotland and northern England in January and February.
